coredata.dc:title: Synergistically driven PdCo alloy based on cross-linked carbon dots for efficient formic acid dehydrogenation
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Han, Sijia
Bian, Linyan
Jiao, Zihao
Liu, Xianyun
Fan, Yanping
Peng, Qiuming
Liu, Baozhong
coredata.dc:description: Modifying the electronic structure of precious metals by alloying with non-precious metals is a proven strategy for enhancing the performance of dehydrogenation catalysts. In this work, a PdCo alloy catalyst supported on N-doped carbon dots (NCDs) was synthesized using a straightforward hydrothermal and reduction process. This catalyst effectively promoted the dehydrogenation of formic acid without the need for any additives at 323 K. The confinement effect of NCDs facilitated the formation of uniformly dispersed PdCo alloy particles (average size of 2.7 nm). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy analysis revealed that the addition of Co not only increases the electron density of Pd but also enhances the electronic support from the electron-rich N atoms in NCDs, thereby significantly improving catalytic activity. Through optimization of the Pd-to-Co molar ratio, it was determined that Pd9Co1/NCDs exhibited superior activity for formic acid dehydrogenation. The turnover frequency of the catalyst was 593 h−1 and the activation energy of the dehydrogenation process was 39.3 kJ·mol−1. This research established an experimental basis for designing noble metal-based catalysts with enhanced catalytic efficiency.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
N-doped
PdCo alloy
Formic acid dehydrogenation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Diagram depicting the preparation method of PdCo/NCDs catalysts.

(a) XRD spectra of NCDs; (b) TEM image and particle size distribution of NCDs; (c) HRTEM image of NCDs; (d) TEM image of Pd9Co1/NCDs; (e) TEM image and particle size distribution of Pd9Co1/NCDs; (f, h) HRTEM image of Pd9Co1/NCDs; (g) EDX element mappings of Pd9Co1/NCDs.

(a) XRD patterns of Pd(10-x)Cox/NCDs; (b) FT-IR spectra of NCDs, Co/NCDs, Pd/NCDs, Pd9Co1/NCDs; (c) XPS full spectrum of Pd9Co1/NCDs, Pd/NCDs, Co/NCDs; (d) High-resolution N 1s XPS spectra of Pd/NCDs, Pd9Co1/NCDs, and (g) the corresponding binding energy shifts; (e) High-resolution Pd 3d XPS spectra of Pd/NCDs, Pd9Co1/NCDs, and (h) the corresponding binding energy shifts; (f) High-resolution Co 2p XPS spectra of Co/NCDs, Pd9Co1/NCDs, and (i) the corresponding binding energy shifts.

(a, b) Hydrogen production profiles of Pd/NCDs, Co/NCDs, Pd9Co1/NCDs and Pd9Co1/XC-72 and corresponding TOF values; (c, d) Hydrogen production profiles and TOF values using different Pd-to-Co molar ratio at 323 K; (e) The hydrogen production profiles at different temperatures of Pd9Co1/NCDs; (f) Arrhenius plot and catalytic performance of Pd9Co1/NCDs; (g, h) Hydrogen production profiles under different FA concentrations and catalyst loadings; (i) Recoverability test of Pd9Co1/NCDs. (error bars represent the standard deviation: n = 5).
Proposed reaction pathway for FA dehydrogenation on Pd9Co1/NCDs catalyst.

head.ce:title: Synergistically driven PdCo alloy based on cross-linked carbon dots for efficient formic acid dehydrogenation

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The PdCo alloy catalyst supported by cross-linked nitrogen-doped carbon dots effectively dehydrogenated formic acid without additives, achieving a turnover frequency of 593 h−1. The introduction of non-precious metal Co atoms enhanced the electron-donating properties of nitrogen in the carbon dots and increased the electron density around Pd, thereby activating formic acid molecules during catalysis.

Modifying the electronic structure of precious metals by alloying with non-precious metals is a proven strategy for enhancing the performance of dehydrogenation catalysts. In this work, a PdCo alloy catalyst supported on N-doped carbon dots (NCDs) was synthesized using a straightforward hydrothermal and reduction process. This catalyst effectively promoted the dehydrogenation of formic acid without the need for any additives at 323 K. The confinement effect of NCDs facilitated the formation of uniformly dispersed PdCo alloy particles (average size of 2.7 nm). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy analysis revealed that the addition of Co not only increases the electron density of Pd but also enhances the electronic support from the electron-rich N atoms in NCDs, thereby significantly improving catalytic activity. Through optimization of the Pd-to-Co molar ratio, it was determined that Pd9Co1/NCDs exhibited superior activity for formic acid dehydrogenation. The turnover frequency of the catalyst was 593 h−1 and the activation energy of the dehydrogenation process was 39.3 kJ·mol−1. This research established an experimental basis for designing noble metal-based catalysts with enhanced catalytic efficiency.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Energy is an important factor in the production of materials and a driving force for economic and social development. Consequently, energy production and availability have a significant impact on the progress of every country. In recent years, the necessity to develop sustainable and environmentally friendly green energy has become more imperative as a result of the overconsumption of fossil fuels and severe environmental degradation. Hydrogen is increasingly recognized as a highly promising green energy source due to its exceptional energy density (142 MJ⋅kg−1) and renewable potential [1–3]. However, the safety and efficiency of hydrogen production and storage technologies continue to be major challenges in the practical implementation of the hydrogen economy [4]. Formic acid (HCOOH or FA) is highly valued for its substantial hydrogen content (53 g hydrogen⋅L−1), minimal toxicity, and convenient storage [5,6]. These properties render it widely recognized as an effective and safe hydrogen storage medium. The two pathways for FA decomposition involve dehydrogenation to produce carbon dioxide (CO2) and hydrogen (H2), as well as dehydration to generate water and carbon monoxide (CO), both of which are dependent on the utilized catalyst. Developing efficient dehydrogenation catalysts is essential for selective FA dehydrogenation (FAD).

Catalysts utilized in FAD reactions can be categorized into two main types: homogeneous and heterogeneous catalysts [6]. Although homogeneous catalysts exhibit high activity, the reaction system relies on organic solvents and additives, which impedes catalyst recovery and thereby greatly reduces their practical application. The advancement of heterogeneous catalysts has emerged as a crucial approach to facilitate the decomposition of FA. The metals reported to have activity for FAD include Rh [7], Ru [8], Au [9], and Pd [10]. Compared to other catalytic systems, Pd-based catalysts exhibit superior performance and high selectivity in the FAD reaction (HCOOH → H2 + CO2) and have consequently received widespread attention [11–13]. However, the high cost of Pd poses a significant challenge to catalyst development, and designing economically viable and highly efficient Pd-based catalysts is crucial for FAD.

The addition of non-precious metals as secondary elements to form alloys with the precious metal Pd is an important approach to enhancing catalytic activity and reducing costs [14–17]. The introduction of a secondary metal can adjust the electronic characteristics of the catalyst material, effectively altering the binding strength of the intermediate compounds, thereby optimizing surface adsorption performance, decreasing H2 adsorption, and enhancing catalytic activity [18,19]. Utilizing metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) as support materials, Ma et al. employed the over-impregnation-low-temperature-reduction method to synthesize Ni0.4Pd0.6/MOF-Cr catalysts [20], which exhibited commendable catalytic performance in FA solution, achieving a turnover frequency (TOF) of 737.9 h−1. The incorporation of the non-precious metal Ni induced a favorable change in the surface electronic state of metallic Pd, thereby enhancing its catalytic activity. Simultaneously, the inclusion of Ni reduced the quantity of the precious metal Pd and overall catalyst cost. Wang et al. prepared a PdCo@Pd/C catalyst loaded with carbon black for FAD, utilizing sodium formate as an accelerator, which enabled efficient FAD at room temperature [21]. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis of the electronic structure of PdCo@Pd/C indicated that the addition of Co can regulate the surface electronic state of Pd, reduce the binding energy of Pd 3d orbitals, and thus enhance the resistance to CO poisoning of active sites on Pd. Introducing a suitable non-precious metal as the second component to form an alloy with the precious metal Pd has been shown to have a beneficial effect on FAD.

The selection of appropriate support materials and optimization of synergistic interactions between the metal catalyst and support are crucial factors in achieving enhanced catalytic activity. Carbon dots (CDs), a type of graphene derivative with sizes of less than 10 nm, have received extensive attention as support materials due to their superior stability, exceptional biocompatibility, large specific surface area, and cost-effectiveness [22–24]. Compared to other carbon materials, the abundant presence of vacancies and surface functional groups (OH, COOH, especially NH2) in CDs offers binding sites for the formation of strong complexes with metal ions (e.g., Ru3+, Pd2+, Co2+, and Cu2+), thereby resulting in the formation of stable metal–carbon composites [25,26]. Liu et al. dissolved melamine, glucose, and ruthenium (III) chloride in 30 mL of pure water and synthesized Ru/N-doped-CDs (NCDs) catalysts through a one-pot hydrothermal method [27]. The interaction between NCDs and Ru regulates the electronic structure of the active Ru site, promoting the formation of the sp2/sp3 interface in N-doped carbon. This interface facilitates water decomposition and balances the adsorption and desorption of H2. Compared to commercial Pt/C, Ru/NCDs exhibited superior H2 evolution reaction performance and stability. Pan et al. synthesized a hydrophilic Ni@NCDs electrocatalyst through a straightforward hydrothermal-pyrolysis approach [28]. The catalysts exhibited exceptional catalytic performance and durability in the electrolysis of water in an alkaline environment to produce H2. Additionally, CDs can serve as a promising photocatalyst after co-doping with non-metal heteroatoms such as N, P, and S or metal heteroatoms like Cu, Zn, and Ti [29]. The introduction of heteroatoms enhances the electron-donating and -accepting capabilities of CDs while facilitating the oxidation–reduction reaction on their surface. In summary, the electron transfer between CDs and metals modulates the electronic configuration of the metal surface, thereby enhancing catalytic performance. The selection of CDs as a support material for loading Pd-based catalysts may potentially enhance FAD efficiency.

In this paper, NCDs were fabricated using a straightforward hydrothermal method. The PdCo/NCDs catalyst was prepared by utilizing NCDs as the support and depositing PdCo alloy onto their surface. The performance of the catalyst in FAD was examined in the absence of additives. The Pd9Co1/NCDs catalyst, obtained by precisely controlling the Pd-to-Co molar ratio, exhibited superior activity (TOF = 593 h−1) in the FAD reaction. The results indicated that the incorporation of the non-precious metal Co can effectively modulate the electronic structure of the active metal Pd. The synergistic interaction between PdCo alloy particles and NCDs can enhance electron transfer and facilitate catalytic reactions. This study is expected to offer a robust approach to designing high-performance alloy catalysts.

Formic acid (CH2O2, 96 %), citric acid monohydrate (C6H8O7·H2O, 99.8 %), cobalt nitrate (Co(NO3)2·6H2O, 99.99 %) and sodium borohydride (NaBH4, 98 %) from Aladdin Industrial Co., Ltd; palladium nitrate (Pd(NO3)2·2H2O, Pd 18.09 wt%) from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd; ethylenediamine (C2H8N2, ≥99 %) from Luoyang Reagent Co. Ltd; Anhydrous ethanol (C2H5OH, AR) from Tianjin Xiehe Co. without any treatment.

Synthesis of NCDs: NCDs were prepared using a hydrothermal approach [30,31]. Citric acid monohydrate (0.075 mol) and ethylenediamine (0.0975 mol) were added to an appropriate amount of deionized water for even mixing, and then the solution was transferred to a high-pressure reaction kettle with a 200 mL capacity made of polytetrafluoroethylene. The mixture was heated at 200 °C for 5 h and cooled naturally to 25 °C. Finally, the solution underwent dialysis in deionized water for 18 h to obtain NCDs.

Synthesis of Pd(10-x)Cox/NCDs: The Pd(10-x)Cox/NCDs catalyst was prepared using the sodium borohydride reduction method. A total of 0.0621 mmol of palladium nitrate and 0.0069 mmol of cobalt nitrate were introduced into 30 mL of NCDs solution under ultrasonic conditions (total theoretical loading amount was 5.0 wt%). Followed by mixing for 30 min to obtain a homogeneous solution. Then 5 mL sodium borohydride solution with a molar ratio of 10:1 to the metal was introduced and vigorously stirred for 4 h. The solids were then separated via centrifugation, dried under vacuum at 30 °C for 12 h. The Pd9Co1/NCDs catalyst was obtained by activation at 300 °C for 1 h under an argon atmosphere. Through these steps, catalysts with different Pd-to-Co molar ratio were prepared, denoted as Pd(10-x)Cox/NCDs (x = 0, 0.5, 1, 3, 5, 7, 10).

The phase composition of the support and catalysts were analyzed using an X-ray powder diffractometer (XRD, SmartLab Rigaku, Japan) within the 2θ range of 10°-90°. The morphologies of catalysts were analyzed and studied using a transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-F200, Japan). The gas composition of FA hydrogen production was analyzed using Thermal Conductivity Detector (TCD, GC-7920, China). The characteristic functional groups of catalysts were assayed by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Nicolet iS50, USA). Analyzing the chemical state of catalyst surface is performed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB 250Xi, USA). The content of Pd and Co elements were obtained by Inductively Coupled Plasma-Optical Emission Spectrometry (ICP-OES, Agilent 5110(OES), USA).

The FAD reaction was conducted via water displacement [11]. Generally, 2 mL of an aqueous solution containing the catalyst is added to a dual-neck round-bottom flask, which is placed in a constant-temperature water bath with magnetic stirring mantained. One of the bottleneck connections is filled with a 100 mL graduated cylinder to measure the volume of gas produced, which was determined by measuring the amount of displaced water. 1.7 mL of FA solution (1.0 M) is rapidly added via a dropper to the other bottleneck to initiate the catalytic reaction. The timing commences with the initial appearance of bubbles, and time is recorded for every 5 mL of gas produced until the reaction reaches completion. The FAD process is carried out in a constant temperature magnetic stirrer (T = 323 K, n = 600 rpm).
The catalyst stability test follows the procedure outlined above. Following each hydrogen evolution, an equal volume of FA is introduced into the system.

Calculate the activation energy (Ea) based on the Arrhenius equation:
(1)
l n
rate
=
E α
RT
+ I n A
Here, the rate represents specific activity, R is the molar gas constant, A denotes the pre-exponential factor, T corresponds to the reaction temperature during catalytic FA hydrogenation (323 K), and Ea is activation energy.

The TOF is determined using the formula provided below:
(2)
TOF =
P 0
V
RT
n
Pd
t 50
-
t 10
Here, P0 represents atmospheric pressure (101325 Pa), V denotes 40 mL (gas generation volume between 10 mL and 50 mL), R corresponds to the molar gas constant, T is 323 K, nPd indicates the theoretical molar amount of Pd in the catalyst, and t 50 and t 10 are the times when gas generation reaches 50 mL and 10 mL respectively.

The synthesis process of PdCo/NCDs catalysts is illustrated in Fig. 1 . Initially, NCDs were prepared via a hydrothermal technique, using ethylenediamine and citric acid as the precursors [30,31]. This reaction primarily involves dehydration polymerization followed by carbonization [23]. Subsequently, Pd and Co metals were loaded on the NCDs through sodium borohydride reduction to fabricate the PdCo/NCDs catalysts.

The structure and morphology of the support material NCDs were investigated using XRD and TEM. As shown in Fig. 2
a, the XRD pattern of NCDs included a broad peak in the 2θ range of 20°–30°, suggesting a high degree of disorder in the carbon atomic structure [26]. Based on the TEM image in Fig. 2 b, NCDs had a uniform particle size distribution with an average particle size (APS) of 2.4 nm. In the high resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) image in Fig. 2 c, the lattice stripe spacing was 0.21 nm, consistent with the (100) crystal plane of graphite carbon, confirming the successful synthesis of NCDs [32].

In the HRTEM image of Pd9Co1/NCDs (Fig. 2 f), distinct lattice fringes of metallic NPs were observed, and the interplanar spacing d was determined as 0.213 nm (Fig. 2 h). This spacing fell within the range of the (111) lattice spacings for Pd (0.224 nm) and Co (0.197 nm), indicating the formation of a metal alloy. The EDX elemental mapping images (Fig. 2 g) revealed that the catalyst comprised five elements, namely C, O, N, Co, and Pd. Notably, Pd and Co exhibited similar distribution regions in support of the presence of a PdCo alloy.

The structural characteristics and functional groups of the catalyst were studied by XRD and FT-IR techniques. The XRD patterns of the Pd(10-x)Cox/NCDs catalysts are depicted in Fig. 3
a. In this figure, catalysts with varying proportions exhibited a broad peak within the 2θ range of 20°-30°, consistent with the diffraction peak corresponding to NCDs in Fig. 2 a. It indicated that NCDs retained the disordered carbon structure after Pd and Co loading. In addition, the diffraction peaks of Pd/NCDs at 2θ = 39.4°, 66.8°, and 80.4° corresponded to the face-centered cubic (1 1 1), (2 2 0), and (3 1 1) crystal planes of Pd (JCPDS: 87-0641), respectively [36]. Compared with Pd/NCDs, the diffraction peak of PdCo/NCDs at 2θ = 39.4° was slightly shifted towards higher angles, indicating that Co incorporation led to a contraction of the face-centered cubic lattice of Pd. This observation further supported the formation of a PdCo alloy [37–39]. No obvious Co diffraction peaks were observed in the XRD spectrum of Co/NCDs, likely because of the high dispersion and small size of Co NPs on NCDs. TEM images ( Fig. S2 ) revealed highly dispersed and small-sized Co particles (1.6 nm), consistent with the XRD findings.

The FT-IR spectra of NCDs, Co/NCDs, Pd/NCDs, and Pd9Co1/NCDs are presented in Fig. 3 b, revealing that NCDs and the synthesized catalysts had characteristic peaks of NH/OH stretching vibrations (3272 cm−1), CH and CO stretching vibrations (2931 cm−1 and 1648 cm−1), and NH and CH bending vibrations (1534 cm−1 and 1428 cm−1). The results indicated that the surfaces of synthesized NCDs were rich in functional groups (NH2, COOH, OH), which can promote the reaction process. Even after metal loading, these functional groups remained intact and continued to enhance the catalytic efficiency of the catalyst during the reaction.

The chemical composition and oxidation states of the catalysts were characterized by XPS. Fig. 3 c presents the full spectra of Pd9Co1/NCDs, Pd/NCDs, and Co/NCDs. All three samples contained three elements, namely C, O, and N. Furthermore, the XPS survey spectrum of Pd/NCDs exhibited a distinct peak corresponding to Pd 3d, while the spectrum of Co/NCDs revealed a peak associated with Co 2p. In the case of Pd9Co1/NCDs, the Pd 3d peak was stronger; conversely, the Co peak was less intense due to the low Co concentration. The average Co loading, determined by ICP-OES ( Table S1 ), was only 0.6 wt%. Fig. S3 presents the full XPS spectrum for the catalyst Pd5Co5/NCDs, which allowed for clearer observation of the Co 2p peak due to the higher proportion of Co. These findings confirmed that both Pd and Co elements were successfully incorporated into NCDs to obtain the Pd9Co1/NCDs catalyst.

Fig. 3 d presents the high-resolution N 1s XPS spectra of the materials. In the spectrum of Pd9Co1/NCDs, the peaks of graphitic N, pyrrolic N, and pyridinic N were observed at 400.9, 400.13, and 398.87 eV, respectively [40,41]. When compared to the corresponding N peak positions (400.72, 399.97, and 398.74 eV) in the spectrum of Pd/NCDs, these peaks were shifted towards higher binding energies (Fig. 3 g), suggesting that the incorporation of the second metal Co facilitates an increased transfer of electrons from the N-rich support NCDs to the metal component, thereby enhancing its catalytic activity. In the high-resolution Pd 3d XPS spectrum of Pd/NCDs (Fig. 3 e), the peaks corresponding to Pd2+ species (337.52 and 342.8 eV) and Pd0 species (335.56 and 340.85 eV) were observed [42]. The detection of Pd2+ can be ascribed to the partial oxidation of metallic Pd during the catalyst preparation process. Notably, after the introduction of Co, both peaks associated with Pd0 and Pd2+ in Pd9Co1/NCDs were shifted towards lower binding energies (Fig. 3 h). In the high-resolution Co 2p XPS spectrum of Pd9Co1/NCDs (Fig. 3 f), a comparison with Co/NCDs (Co0: 774.5 eV, Co2+: 781.1 eV, Co3+: 782.8 eV, satellite: 786.5 eV) revealed that the peaks corresponding to Co 2p shifted towards higher binding energies (Co0: 775.87 eV, Co2+: 781.42 eV, Co3+: 783.86 eV, satellite: 787.34 eV) (Fig. 3 i). These findings suggest an electron transfer between Pd and Co in Pd9Co1/NCDs, thereby confirming the formation of PdCo alloy. Notably, the addition of Co enhanced the electron density surrounding Pd, which may contribute to an enhanced catalytic FAD performance [43].

The catalytic performance of catalysts in additive-free FAD was investigated through a series of intermittent reaction experiments. Fig. 4
a and b depict the H2 production profiles of various catalysts in the FAD process and the TOF values at 323 K. Pd9Co1/XC-72 exhibited limited catalytic activity in FAD, yielding only 15 mL H2 within 16 min (TOF = 69 h−1). Conversely, Pd9Co1/NCDs demonstrated exceptional catalytic efficiency in FAD achieving the generation of 65 mL H2 within just 5 min and a remarkable TOF value of 593 h−1. The results indicated that, despite both NCDs and XC-72 being carbon materials, the distinctive quantum dot effect of NCDs and their anchoring effect on metal as support materials enhance the active sites of Pd [27,44]. Simultaneously, during the reaction, N atoms in NCDs provide additional electrons to Pd, resulting in exceptional catalytic performance of the Pd9Co1/NCDs catalyst. Notably, Co/NCDs did not exhibit any catalytic FAD activity, while Pd/NCDs displayed favorable activity (TOF = 337 h−1), although lower than that of Pd9Co1/NCDs. This observation suggested that Pd functions as the main active site to promote the reaction, while Co plays a supporting role by providing extra electrons and improving overall catalyst efficiency. In addition, the dehydrogenation products of FA on the catalyst Pd9Co1/NCDs were analyzed by gas chromatography. The generated signals corresponded to H2 and CO2, and no CO signal was detected ( Fig. S4 ), indicating that the Pd9Co1/NCDs catalyst has 100 % selectivity for FAD.

Fig. 4 c shows the effect of the Pd-to-Co molar ratio on catalytic performance at 323 K. The findings confirmed that the catalytic performance exhibited a volcano-shaped trend, marked by a reduction in Pd content and an increase in Co content (Fig. 4 d). Notably, the Pd(10-x)Cox/NCDs catalysts exhibited the highest catalytic activity at a Pd-to-Co molar ratio of 9:1. The H2 evolution curves and catalytic activity of Pd9Co1/NCDs at different temperatures are depicted in Fig. 4 e and f. Increasing the reaction temperature enhanced the catalytic activity, which can be attributed to the increased collision probability between effective molecules with rising temperatures, thereby enhancing molecular reactivity and accelerating reactions within the system. According to Eq. (1), the Ea under Pd9Co1/NCDs catalysis was 39.3 kJ⋅mol−1. More comparative results of the TOF and Ea values of Pd-based catalysts are presented in Table S2.

The effect of FA quantity and catalyst dosage on catalytic activity was investigated. The dehydrogenation reaction was conducted using Pd9Co1/NCDs and varying molar amounts of FA (5.25, 3.50, 1.75, and 0.88 mmol) at a reaction temperature of 323 K. As shown in Fig. 4 g, the volume of produced H2 increased with increasing FA amount, demonstrating the potential for the use of this catalyst in industrial applications. The corresponding TOF values were 671, 631, 593, and 588 h−1, respectively ( Fig. S5 ). The curves depicting H2 production through FAD using varying amounts of catalyst (0.031, 0.062, 0.093, 0.124, and 0.155 mmol) and the corresponding TOF values are provided in Figs. 4h and S6. With an increase in the amount of Pd9Co1/NCDs, the rate of FAD gradually improved, resulting in a considerable increase in TOF from 402 to 1267 h−1. This enhancement is mainly due to the greater amount of catalysts and thereby the higher number of active sites.

Based on the above results, in combination with our previous density functional theory studies on the FAD mechanism over Pd/CDs catalysts [11], we suggest the mechanism of FAD on the Pd9Co1/NCDs catalyst as shown in Fig. 5 . The dehydrogenation process involves three reaction steps:
HCOOH + 2Pd9Co1* → Pd9Co1-[HCOO]*+Pd9Co1-[H]*(3)
Pd9Co1-[HCOO]* → CO2 + Pd9Co1-[H]*(4)
Pd9Co1-[H]*+Pd9Co1-[H]* → 2Pd9Co1*+H2(5)

First, HCOOH molecules adsorb on the surface of NCDs with rich functional groups and migrate to the surface of Pd9Co1 NPs. The OH bond of the HCOOH molecule breaks to obtain Pd9Co1-[H]* and form a Pd9Co1-[HCOO]* intermediate. The alkaline sites generated by abundant N species within NCDs facilitate the cleavage of the OH bond. Subsequently, the intermediate undergoes cleavage of the CH bond to yield Pd9Co1-[H]* and COO*; the incorporation of Co enhances the electron-rich environment around Pd, thereby facilitating the efficient progression of this step. Finally, two Pd9Co1-[H]* species couple to generate an H2 molecule which desorbs rapidly from the Pd9Co1 surface, while COO* is converted into CO2. Concurrently, the catalyst's active center is regenerated for subsequent reactions. The FAD process entails the cleavage of the OH bond in HCOOH and the CH bond in [HCOO]* to generate two H species that subsequently combine to form H2 molecules [45]. Among these steps, the cleavage of the CH bond in [HCOO]* is one of the key steps [11,46–48], which can be facilitated by a PdCo alloy enriched with electron donors. The N-doped sites within NCDs enhance the dissociation of the OH bond in HCOOH during FA deprotonation, while the abundant surface functional groups provide active adsorption sites for this reaction [45]. Importantly, NCDs also play a vital role in stabilizing the PdCo NPs.

In summary, this study successfully immobilized PdCo nanoparticles (NPs) onto N-doped carbon dots (NCDs) via the sodium borohydride reduction method, resulting in the preparation of the Pd(10-x)Cox/NCDs catalyst. Without the addition of any auxiliary agent, the catalyst effectively facilitated the formic acid dehydrogenation (FAD) in aqueous media. The PdCo NPs supported on NCDs exhibited a small particle size (2.7 nm) and excellent dispersion characteristics. The Pd9Co1/NCDs catalyst, optimized through precise tuning of the metal ratio, demonstrated the highest activity for FAD with a turnover frequency of 593 h−1. The activation energy calculated using the Arrhenius equation was determined to be 39.3 kJ·mol−1, indicating that cross-linked NCDs constrain the size of PdCo NPs while providing additional active adsorption sites due to their rich surface functional groups. Furthermore, the alkaline sites generated by nitrogen doping facilitate HCOOH bond cleavage during FAD reactions. Incorporating the non-precious metal Co modifies the electronic state surrounding active Pd, leading to an increased electron density around this metal. The enhanced electron density facilitates the formation and cleavage of the [HCOO]* bond during FAD and consequently improves catalytic activity.

In previous studies on FAD catalysts based on carbon dots (CDs), the relatively low catalytic efficiency caused researchers to focus primarily on noble metals (such as Pd, PdAg, and PdAu) [11,30]. In this study, the non-noble metal Co was introduced into a CDs-supported catalyst, achieving a bidirectional Pd activation strategy of using CDs as support and Co as auxiliary metal, significantly enhancing the catalytic efficiency of active sites while potentially reducing catalyst costs. Future research should explore other non-noble metals (Cu, Cr, and others) and additional doping elements (B, P, and others) to further optimize and enhance catalyst performance through this bidirectional activation approach. This work provides a viable strategy for improving the performance of Pd-based alloys and promoting formic acid as an effective liquid material for hydrogen storage in energy and environmental applications.

Sijia Han: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Linyan Bian: Writing – review & editing, Conceptualization. Zihao Jiao: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis. Xianyun Liu: Investigation. Yanping Fan: Supervision, Conceptualization. Qiuming Peng: Supervision, Conceptualization. Baozhong Liu: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research is financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (U22A20120 and 52071135), the Natural Science Foundation of Hebei Province for Innovation Groups Program (C2022203003) and Fundamental Research Funds for the Universities of Henan Province (NSFRF220201), Key Scientific and Technological Project of Henan Province (242102241032).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: TEM images, XPS spectra, XRD patterns, Gas chromatogram, FA hydrogen production TOF value images, ICP-OES table, comparison table of various catalysts reported in literature for hydrogen production from FA. Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2025.02.120.

ce:section.ce:para:
